title: Finding probabilities with sample proportions
description: Practice finding probabilities involving the sampling distribution of a sample proportion.
topic_path: Sampling distribution of a sample proportion

assessment_items.question_content:
Suppose that $15\%$ of the $1750$ students at a school have experienced extreme levels of stress during the past month. A high school newspaper doesn't know this figure, but they are curious what it is, so they decide to ask a simple random sample of $160$ students if they have experienced extreme levels of stress during the past month. Subsequently, they find that $10\%$ of the sample replied “yes” to the question. 

**Assuming the true proportion is $15\%$, what is the approximate probability that more than $10\%$ of the sample would report that they experienced extreme levels of stress during the past month?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample proportion $\hat{p}$. So we need to identify the mean $\mu_{\hat p}$, standard deviation $\sigma_{\hat p}$, and shape of the sampling distribution in question.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

The actual proportion of students at the school who experienced extreme levels of stress during the past month is $0.15$.

So we know that the mean of the sampling distribution is

$\mu_{\hat p}=p=0.15$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

Since the sample size of $n=160$ students is less than $10\%$ of the population of $1750$ students at the school, we can assume independence between students in the sample and find the standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

$\begin{align}
\sigma_{\hat{p}}&=\sqrt{\dfrac{p\left(1-p\right)} {n}} \\\\
&=\sqrt{\dfrac{0.15\left(0.85\right)}{160}} \\\\
&\approx0.0282
\end{align}$

###The shape of the sampling distribution

Furthermore, the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}$ is approximately normal since the criteria below are both met.

$\begin{align}
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected successes:} \\\\
&np=160\left(0.15\right)=24\geq10 \\\\
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected failures:} \\\\
&n\left(1-p\right)=160\left(1-0.15\right)=136\ge10
\end{align}$

###Calculating $P(\hat p>0.10)$

We established that $\hat p$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\hat p>0.10$:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on an x axis representing p hat that goes from 0.07 to 0.23. The mean, or mu sub p hat, = 0.15. The standard deviations, or sigma sub p hat, approximately equals 0.028. The value p hat = 0.10 is marked. The area under the curve to the right of 0.10 is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/026f355caae82468d6038bce58fb13f564d76f96)

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 0.10 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 1.00 \\\\
&\mu=0.15 \\\\
&\sigma=0.028
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx 0.9629$

###Answer

$P(\hat p>0.10) \approx 0.96$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
The United Kingdom Forestry Commission reports that $43\%$ of the $3.16$ million hectares of woodland area in the United Kingdom had certification identifying them as "sustainably managed" in 2016. Suppose an employee took a simple random sample of $400$ of the hectares and saw that the records showed that $47\%$ of the sampled hectares had that certification in 2016.

[//]: (https://www.forestry.gov.uk/forestry/infd-7aqdgc)

**Assuming that the Forestry Commission's report is accurate, what is the approximate probability that more than $47\%$ of the sample would have had the certification in 2016?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample proportion $\hat{p}$. So we need to identify the mean $\mu_{\hat p}$, standard deviation $\sigma_{\hat p}$, and shape of the sampling distribution in question.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

If we assume that the Forestry Commission's $43\%$ claim is correct, then the actual proportion of hectares of woodland area which have certification identifying them as “sustainably managed” is $0.43$.

So we know that the mean of the sampling distribution is:

$\mu_{\hat p}=p=0.43$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

Since the sample size of $n=400$ hectares is less than $10\%$ of the population of $3.16$ million hectares, we can assume independence between hectares in the sample and find the standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

$\begin{align}\sigma_{\hat{p}}&=\sqrt{\dfrac{p\left(1-p\right)}{n}}\\\\
&=\sqrt{\dfrac{0.43\left(0.57\right)}{400}}\\\\
&\approx 0.0248
\end{align}$

###The shape of the sampling distribution

Furthermore, the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}$ is approximately normal since the criteria below are both met.

$\begin{align}
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected successes:} \\\\
&np=400\left(0.43\right)=172\geq10 \\\\
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected failures:} \\\\
&n\left(1-p\right)=400\left(1-0.43\right)=228\ge10
\end{align}$

###Calculating $P(\hat p>0.47)$

We established that $\hat p$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\hat p>0.47$:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on an x axis representing p hat that goes from 0.36 to 0.50. The mean, or mu sub p hat, = 0.43. The standard deviations, or sigma sub p hat, approximately equals 0.025. The value p hat = 0.47 is marked. The area under the curve to the right of 0.47 is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/1d30f3f07127040c5e97380986b4ca6802006184)

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 0.47 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 1.00 \\\\
&\mu=0.43 \\\\
&\sigma=0.025
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx 0.0548$

###Answer

$P(\hat p>0.47) \approx 0.05$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Beatriz takes a simple random sample of $350$ orders from the more than $5000$ total orders to her store and finds that $12\%$ of the sampled orders were returned. 

**Assuming that it is really $9\%$ of all orders to her store which were returned, what is the approximate probability that more than $12\%$ of the sampled orders would have been returned?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample proportion $\hat{p}$. So we need to identify the mean $\mu_{\hat p}$, standard deviation $\sigma_{\hat p}$, and shape of the sampling distribution in question.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

We are given that the actual proportion of orders to her store which were returned is $0.09$.

So we know that the mean of the sampling distribution is:

$\mu_{\hat p}=p=0.09$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

Since the sample size of $n=350$ orders is less than $10\%$ of the population of over $5000$ orders, we can assume independence between orders in the sample and find the standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

$\begin{align}\sigma_{\hat{p}}&=\sqrt{\dfrac{p\left(1-p\right)}{n}}\\\\
&=\sqrt{\dfrac{0.09\left(0.91\right)}{350}}\\\\
&\approx 0.0153
\end{align}$

###The shape of the sampling distribution

Furthermore, the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}$ is approximately normal since the criteria below are both met.

$\begin{align}
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected successes:} \\\\
&np=350\left(0.09\right)=31.5\geq10 \\\\
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected failures:} \\\\
&n\left(1-p\right)=350\left(1-0.09\right)=318.5\geq10
\end{align}$

###Calculating $P(\hat p>0.12)$

We established that $\hat p$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\hat p>0.12$:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on an x axis representing p hat that goes from 0.044 to 0.136. The mean, or mu sub p hat, = 0.09. The standard deviations, or sigma sub p hat, approximately equals 0.015. The value p hat = 0.12 is marked. The area under the curve to the right of 0.12 is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7bc30fd1992b4e1ceefa82750e9969f06f983483)

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 0.12 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 1.00 \\\\
&\mu=0.09 \\\\
&\sigma=0.015
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx 0.02275$

###Answer

$P(\hat p>0.12) \approx 0.02$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A consulting agency reports that only $30\%$ of a company's website users can successfully use a certain feature of the website. Skeptical of this claim, one of the website developers takes a simple random sample of $150$ of the company's approximately $8000$ users and tests whether they can use the feature successfully. The developer finds that $36\%$ of the sampled users use the feature successfully.

**Assuming the agency's $30\%$ claim is correct, what is the approximate probability that more than $36\%$ of the sample would use the feature successfully?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample proportion $\hat{p}$. So we need to identify the mean $\mu_{\hat p}$, standard deviation $\sigma_{\hat p}$, and shape of the sampling distribution in question.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

If we assume that the consulting agency's $30\%$ claim is correct, then the actual proportion of the company's users who use the feature successfully is $0.3$.

So we know that the mean of the sampling distribution is:

$\mu_{\hat p}=p=0.3$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

Since the sample size of $n=150$ users is less than $10\%$ of the population of approximately $8000$ users, we can assume independence between users in the sample and find the standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

$\begin{align}\sigma_{\hat{p}}&=\sqrt{\dfrac{p\left(1-p\right)}{n}}\\\\
&=\sqrt{\dfrac{0.3\left(0.7\right)}{150}}\\\\
&\approx 0.0374
\end{align}$

###The shape of the sampling distribution

Furthermore, the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}$ is approximately normal since the criteria below are both met.

$\begin{align}
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected successes:} \\\\
&np=150\left(0.3\right)=45\geq10 \\\\
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected failures:} \\\\
&n\left(1-p\right)=150\left(1-0.3\right)=105\ge10
\end{align}$

###Calculating $P\left(\hat p>0.36\right)$

We established that $\hat p$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\hat p>0.36$:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on an x axis representing p hat that goes from 0.19 to 0.41. The mean, or mu sub p hat, = 0.3. The standard deviations, or sigma sub p hat, approximately equals 0.037. The value p hat = 0.36 is marked. The area under the curve to the right of 0.36 is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f7e009ce54c8debe9c1b571480a3fc6e3436f495)

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 0.36\\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 1.00 \\\\
&\mu=0.3 \\\\
&\sigma=0.037
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx 0.0524$

###Answer

$P\left(\hat p>0.36\right) \approx 0.05$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A local agricultural cooperative claims that $55\%$ of about $60{,}000$ adults in a county believe that gardening should be part of the school curriculum. However, when you take a simple random sample of $300$ of the adults in the county, only $50\%$ say that they believe that gardening should be part of the school curriculum.

**Assuming that the agricultural cooperative's claim is accurate, what is the approximate probability that less than $50\%$ of the sample would say that they believe that gardening should be part of the school curriculum?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample proportion $\hat{p}$. So we need to identify the mean $\mu_{\hat p}$, standard deviation $\sigma_{\hat p}$, and shape of the sampling distribution in question.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

If we assume that the cooperative's $55\%$ claim is correct, then the actual proportion of adults in the county who believe that gardening should be part of the school curriculum is $0.55$.

So we know that the mean of the sampling distribution is:

$\mu_{\hat p}=p=0.55$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

Since the sample size of $n=300$ adults is less than $10\%$ of the population of the about $60{,}000$ adults in the county, we can assume independence between adults in the sample and find the standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

$\begin{align}\sigma_{\hat{p}}&=\sqrt{\dfrac{p\left(1-p\right)}{n}}\\\\
&=\sqrt{\dfrac{0.55\left(0.45\right)}{300}}\\\\
&\approx 0.0287
\end{align}$

###The shape of the sampling distribution

Furthermore, the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}$ is approximately normal since the criteria below are both met.

$\begin{align}
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected successes:} \\\\
&np=300\left(0.55\right)=165\geq10 \\\\
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected failures:} \\\\
&n\left(1-p\right)=300\left(1-0.55\right)=135\ge10
\end{align}$

###Calculating $P(\hat p<0.5)$

We established that $\hat p$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\hat p<0.5$:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on an x axis representing p hat that goes from 0.46 to 0.64. The mean, or mu sub p hat, = 0.55. The standard deviations, or sigma sub p hat, approximately equals 0.029. The value p hat = 0.50 is marked. The area under the curve to the left of 0.50 is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/49975ca59a3483a18b03e940b7d6131e8615cc49)

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 0.00 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 0.5 \\\\
&\mu=0.55 \\\\
&\sigma=0.029
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx 0.0423$

###Answer

$P(\hat p<0.5) \approx 0.04$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
According to a survey, $63\%$ of the Scottish population has visited woodland in the previous year. Skeptical about this claim, you decide to take a simple random sample of $650$ people in this population, and you ask them if they had visited woodland in the previous year. You find that $60\%$ of the sample replied "yes" to your question.

**Assuming that the original survey's $63\%$ claim is correct, what is the approximate probability that less than $60\%$ of the sample would report that they had visited woodland in the previous year?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample proportion $\hat{p}$. So we need to identify the mean $\mu_{\hat p}$, standard deviation $\sigma_{\hat p}$, and shape of the sampling distribution in question.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

If we assume that the survey's $63\%$ claim is correct, then the actual proportion of people from the Scottish population who visited woodland in the previous year is $0.63$.

So we know that the mean of the sampling distribution is:

$\mu_{\hat p}=p=0.63$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

It's safe to assume that the sample of $n=650$ people is less than $10\%$ of the population of all the people in Scotland, so we can assume independence between people in the sample and find the standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

$\begin{align}\sigma_{\hat{p}}&=\sqrt{\dfrac{p\left(1-p\right)}{n}}\\\\
&=\sqrt{\dfrac{0.63\left(0.37\right)}{650}}\\\\
&\approx 0.0189
\end{align}$

###The shape of the sampling distribution

Furthermore, the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}$ is approximately normal since the criteria below are both met.

$\begin{align}
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected successes:} \\\\
&np=650\left(0.63\right)=409.5\geq10 \\\\
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected failures:} \\\\
&n\left(1-p\right)=650\left(1-0.63\right)=240.5\ge10
\end{align}$

###Calculating $P(\hat p<0.60)$

We established that $\hat p$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\hat p<0.60$:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on an x axis representing p hat that goes from 0.57 to 0.69. The mean, or mu sub p hat, = 0.63. The standard deviations, or sigma sub p hat, approximately equals 0.019. The value p hat = 0.60 is marked. The area under the curve to the left of 0.60 is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/45244319299d52a02f16676562e84659ea8fa1c7)

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 0.00 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 0.60 \\\\
&\mu=0.63 \\\\
&\sigma=0.019
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx 0.05717$

###Answer

$P(\hat p<0.60) \approx 0.06$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
An article written for a magazine claims that $78\%$ of the magazine's subscribers report eating healthily the previous day. Suppose we select a simple random sample of $675$ of the magazine's approximately $50{,}000$ subscribers to check the accuracy of this claim.

**Assuming the article's $78\%$ claim is correct, what is the approximate probability that less than $80\%$ of the sample would report eating healthily the previous day?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample proportion $\hat{p}$. So we need to identify the mean $\mu_{\hat p}$, standard deviation $\sigma_{\hat p}$, and shape of the sampling distribution in question.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

If we assume that the article's $78\%$ claim is correct, then the actual proportion of the magazine's subscribers who report eating healthily the previous day is $0.78$.

So we know that the mean of the sampling distribution is:

$\mu_{\hat p}=p=0.78$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

Since the sample size of $n=675$ subscribers is less than $10\%$ of the population of approximately $50{,}000$ subscribers, we can assume independence between subscribers in the sample and find the standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

$\begin{align}\sigma_{\hat{p}}&=\sqrt{\dfrac{p\left(1-p\right)}{n}}\\\\
&=\sqrt{\dfrac{0.78\left(0.22\right)}{675}}\\\\
&\approx 0.0159
\end{align}$

###The shape of the sampling distribution

Furthermore, the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}$ is approximately normal since the criteria below are both met.

$\begin{align}
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected successes:} \\\\
&np=675\left(0.78\right)=526.5\geq10 \\\\
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected failures:} \\\\
&n\left(1-p\right)=675\left(1-0.78\right)=148.5\ge10
\end{align}$

###Calculating $P(\hat p<0.80)$

We established that $\hat p$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\hat p<0.80$:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on an x axis representing p hat that goes from 0.73 to 0.83. The mean, or mu sub p hat, = 0.78. The standard deviations, or sigma sub p hat, approximately equals 0.016. The value p hat = 0.80 is marked. The area under the curve to the left of 0.80 is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3957f0313273c3b12c9a8a29ce27d40d77df489e)

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 0.00 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 0.80 \\\\
&\mu=0.78 \\\\
&\sigma=0.016
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx 0.8944$

###Answer

$P(\hat p<0.80) \approx 0.89$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
An article claimed that only $38\%$ of hotel visitors used the alarm clock provided. A hotel manager wanted to see whether that proportion applied to the hotel where she worked, so she took a random sample of $700$ of the approximately $10{,}000$ visitors to the hotel one month and checked whether they used their room alarm clock. Of the sampled visitors, only $35\%$ used the alarm clock.

**Assuming that the article's $38\%$ claim is also the true proportion for visitors to the hotel, what is the approximate probability that less than $35\%$ of the sample used the alarm clock?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample proportion $\hat{p}$. So we need to identify the mean $\mu_{\hat p}$, standard deviation $\sigma_{\hat p}$, and shape of the sampling distribution in question.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

If we assume that the article's $38\%$ claim is also accurate for the particular hotel and month, then the actual proportion of visitors to that hotel who use the room alarm clock that month is $0.38$.

So we know that the mean of the sampling distribution is:

$\mu_{\hat p}=p=0.38$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

Since the sample size of $n=700$ visitors is less than $10\%$ of the population of approximately $10{,}000$ visitors, we can assume independence between visitors in the sample and find the standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

$\begin{align}\sigma_{\hat{p}}&=\sqrt{\dfrac{p\left(1-p\right)}{n}}\\\\
&=\sqrt{\dfrac{0.38\left(0.62\right)}{700}}\\\\
&\approx 0.0183
\end{align}$

###The shape of the sampling distribution

Furthermore, the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}$ is approximately normal since the criteria below are both met.

$\begin{align}
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected successes:} \\\\
&np=700\left(0.38\right)=266\geq10 \\\\
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected failures:} \\\\
&n\left(1-p\right)=700\left(1-0.38\right)=434\ge10
\end{align}$

###Calculating $P(\hat p<0.35)$

We established that $\hat p$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $\hat p<0.35$:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on an x axis representing p hat that goes from 0.32 to 0.44. The mean, or mu sub p hat, = 0.38. The standard deviations, or sigma sub p hat, approximately equals 0.018. The value p hat = 0.35 is marked. The area under the curve to the left of 0.35 is shaded.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/751028cecd36d3f0e314010b8c83175b905c5ddb)

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 0.00 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 0.35 \\\\
&\mu=0.38 \\\\
&\sigma=0.018
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx 0.0478$

###Answer

$P(\hat p<0.35) \approx 0.05$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
A camp accrediting association has $2{,}400$ camps in their association. They claim that $45\%$ of their camps offer specialized programs for individuals with disabilities. Suppose you contact a simple random sample of $200$ of the camps and ask them whether they offer specialized programs for individuals with disabilities. 

**Assuming that the association's claim is correct, what is the approximate probability that between $40\%$ and $50\%$ of the camps reply "yes" to your question?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample proportion $\hat{p}$. So we need to identify the mean $\mu_{\hat p}$, standard deviation $\sigma_{\hat p}$, and shape of the sampling distribution in question.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

If we assume that the association's $45\%$ claim is correct, then the actual proportion of camps which offer specialized programs for individuals with disabilities is $0.45$.

So we know that the mean of the sampling distribution is:

$\mu_{\hat p}=p=0.45$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

Since the sample size of $n=200$ camps is less than $10\%$ of the population of $2{,}400$ camps, we can assume independence between camps in the sample and find the standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

$\begin{align}\sigma_{\hat{p}}&=\sqrt{\dfrac{p\left(1-p\right)}{n}}\\\\
&=\sqrt{\dfrac{0.45\left(0.55\right)}{200}}\\\\
&\approx 0.0352
\end{align}$

###The shape of the sampling distribution

Furthermore, the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}$ is approximately normal since the criteria below are both met.

$\begin{align}
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected successes:} \\\\
&np=200\left(0.45\right)=90\geq10 \\\\
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected failures:} \\\\
&n\left(1-p\right)=200\left(1-0.45\right)=110\ge10
\end{align}$

###Calculating $P(0.4<\hat p<0.5)$

We established that $\hat p$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $0.4<\hat p<0.5$:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on an x axis representing p hat that goes from 0.34 to 0.56. The mean, or mu sub p hat, = 0.45. The standard deviations, or sigma sub p hat, approximately equals 0.035. The values 0.40 and 0.50 are marked. The area under the curve between the two values is shaded representing the probability of 0.40 less than p hat less than 0.50.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/97b4b02b1c2b857ec4c049398746c2b6433c5e13)

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 0.4 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 0.5 \\\\
&\mu=0.45 \\\\
&\sigma=0.035
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx 0.84687$

###Answer

$P(0.4<\hat p<0.5) \approx 0.84$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
The Department of Tourism reported that in May of 2017, approximately $63\%$ of the tourists to the Philippines were from Asia. They also noted that it was the first time that the Philippines had more than $500{,}000$ tourists in the month of May. Suppose another organization had taken a simple random sample of $600$ of the tourists in that population.

**Assuming that the reported $63\%$ claim is accurate, what is the approximate probability that the other organization's results were within $2$ percentage points of the Department of Tourism's results?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample proportion $\hat{p}$. So we need to identify the mean $\mu_{\hat p}$, standard deviation $\sigma_{\hat p}$, and shape of the sampling distribution in question.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

If we assume that the Department of Tourism's $63\%$ claim is correct, then the actual proportion of tourists to the Philippines who were from Asia is $0.63$.

So we know that the mean of the sampling distribution is:

$\mu_{\hat p}=p=0.63$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

Since the sample size of $n=600$ tourists is less than $10\%$ of the population of over $500{,}000$ tourists, we can assume independence between tourists in the sample and find the standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

$\begin{align}\sigma_{\hat{p}}&=\sqrt{\dfrac{p\left(1-p\right)}{n}}\\\\
&=\sqrt{\dfrac{0.63\left(0.37\right)}{600}}\\\\
&\approx 0.0197
\end{align}$

###The shape of the sampling distribution

Furthermore, the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}$ is approximately normal since the criteria below are both met.

$\begin{align}
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected successes:} \\\\
&np=600\left(0.63\right)=378\geq10 \\\\
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected failures:} \\\\
&n\left(1-p\right)=600\left(1-0.63\right)=222\ge10
\end{align}$

###Calculating $P(0.61<\hat p<0.65)$

We want the probability that their results were within $2$ percentage points of the Department of Tourism's claim, so we are looking for $P(0.61<\hat p < 0.65)$. We established that $\hat p$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $0.61 <\hat p<0.65$:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on an x axis representing p hat that goes from 0.57 to 0.69. The mean, or mu sub p hat, = 0.63. The standard deviations, or sigma sub p hat, approximately equals 0.020. The values 0.61 and 0.65 are marked. The area under the curve between the two values is shaded representing the probability of 0.61 less than p hat less than 0.65.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6ee67e53912866b5070928f56dc14363eb2da5ec)

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 0.61 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 0.65 \\\\
&\mu=0.63 \\\\
&\sigma=0.020
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx 0.68269$

###Answer

$P(0.61<\hat p<0.65) \approx 0.69$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
The school board claims that $85\%$ of the over $1{,}600$ families with children in their district send their children to public school. Skeptical of this claim, a private school administrator takes a simple random sample of $120$ families with children in the district and asks them whether they send their children to public school.

**Assuming the school board's claim is correct, what is the probability that the administrator's results are within $5$ percentage points of the school board's $85\%$ claim?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample proportion $\hat{p}$. So we need to identify the mean $\mu_{\hat p}$, standard deviation $\sigma_{\hat p}$, and shape of the sampling distribution in question.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

If we assume that the school board's $85\%$ claim is correct, then the actual proportion of families with children in the district who send their children to public school is $0.85$.

So we know that the mean of the sampling distribution is:

$\mu_{\hat p}=p=0.85$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

Since the sample size of $n=120$ families is less than $10\%$ of the population of over $1{,}600$ families, we can assume independence between families in the sample and find the standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

$\begin{align}\sigma_{\hat{p}}&=\sqrt{\dfrac{p\left(1-p\right)}{n}}\\\\
&=\sqrt{\dfrac{0.85\left(0.15\right)}{120}}\\\\
&\approx 0.0326
\end{align}$

###The shape of the sampling distribution

Furthermore, the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}$ is approximately normal since the criteria below are both met.

$\begin{align}
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected successes:} \\\\
&np=120\left(0.85\right)=102\geq10 \\\\
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected failures:} \\\\
&n\left(1-p\right)=120\left(1-0.85\right)=18\ge10
\end{align}$

###Calculating $P(0.8<\hat p<0.9)$

We want the probability to be within $5$ percentage points of the school board's claim, so we are looking for $P(0.8<\hat p < 0.9)$. We established that $\hat p$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $0.8 <\hat p<0.9$:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on an x axis representing p hat that goes from 0.80 to 0.90. The mean, or mu sub p hat, = 0.85. The standard deviations, or sigma sub p hat, approximately equals 0.033. The values 0.80 and 0.90 are marked. The area under the curve between the two values is shaded representing the probability of 0.80 less than p hat less than 0.90.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/81bc489b33ef9351e8b8d891b95eb8ac6c31c33d)

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 0.8 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 0.9 \\\\
&\mu=0.85 \\\\
&\sigma=0.033
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx 0.8703$

###Answer

$P(0.8<\hat p<0.9) \approx 0.87$

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
According to the 2011 National Survey of Fishing, Hunting, and Wildlife-Associated Recreation, there were over $71$ million wildlife watchers in the US. Of these wildlife watchers, the survey reports that $80\%$ actively observed mammals. Suppose that one of the census workers repeated the survey with a simple random sample of only $500$ wildlife watchers that same year.

**Assuming that the original survey's $80\%$ claim is correct, what is the approximate probability that between $79\%$ and $81\%$ of the $500$ sampled wildlife watchers actively observed mammals in 2011?**

[//]: (https://www.census.gov/prod/2012pubs/fhw11-qkfact.pdf)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
###The sampling distribution

This question involves a sampling distribution of a sample proportion $\hat{p}$. So we need to identify the mean $\mu_{\hat p}$, standard deviation $\sigma_{\hat p}$, and shape of the sampling distribution in question.

###The mean of the sampling distribution

If we assume that the original survey's $80\%$ claim is correct, then the actual proportion of US wildlife watchers who actively observed mammals in 2011 is $0.8$.

So we know that the mean of the sampling distribution is:

$\mu_{\hat p}=p=0.8$

###The standard deviation of the sampling distribution

Since the sample size of $n=500$ watchers is less than $10\%$ of the population of over $71$ million watchers, we can assume independence between watchers in the sample and find the standard deviation of the sampling distribution:

$\begin{align}\sigma_{\hat{p}}&=\sqrt{\dfrac{p\left(1-p\right)}{n}}\\\\
&=\sqrt{\dfrac{0.8\left(0.2\right)}{500}}\\\\
&\approx 0.0179
\end{align}$

###The shape of the sampling distribution

Furthermore, the sampling distribution of $\hat{p}$ is approximately normal since the criteria below are both met.

$\begin{align}
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected successes:} \\\\
&np=500\left(0.8\right)=400\geq10 \\\\
&\text{At least } 10 \text{ expected failures:} \\\\
&n\left(1-p\right)=500\left(1-0.8\right)=100\ge10
\end{align}$

###Calculating $P(0.79<\hat p<0.81)$

We established that $\hat p$ is normally distributed, and we found its mean and standard deviation, so we can use the "normalcdf" function on most graphing calculators to find the probability that $0.79 <\hat p<0.81$:

![A normal distribution curve is plotted on an x axis representing p hat that goes from 0.75 to 0.85. The mean, or mu sub p hat, = 0.8. The standard deviations, or sigma sub p hat, approximately equals 0.018. The values 0.79 and 0.81 are marked. The area under the curve between the two values is shaded representing the probability of 0.79 less than p hat less than 0.81.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0c6fbb6564b171005140da711b2a14d643934f04)

$\begin{align}
&\text{normalcdf:} \\\\
&\text{lower bound: } 0.79 \\\\
&\text{upper bound: } 0.81 \\\\
&\mu=0.8 \\\\
&\sigma=0.018
\end{align}$

Output:  
$\approx 0.4215$

###Answer

$P(0.79<\hat p<0.81) \approx 0.42$

[[☃ explanation 1]]
